title: The Algorithmic Crash
summary: A transport AI caused a fatal crash after executives allegedly ignored safety warnings.

preview.summary: A transport AI caused a fatal crash after executives allegedly ignored safety warnings.
preview.charge: Executives are accused of criminal negligence after deploying unsafe transport AI.
preview.evidence: Internal testing, launch approvals, and known braking failures form the backbone of the prosecution case.
preview.complication: Responsibility is split across executives, engineers, regulators, and outside sensor dependencies.

policeReport: A self-driving passenger shuttle failed to brake at an urban merge and struck another vehicle, causing one death and several serious injuries. Investigators recovered internal safety reports describing unresolved edge-case braking failures and approval emails showing the launch moved forward despite requests for additional testing.

witnesses.name: Maya Ross
witnesses.statement: I flagged inconsistent braking performance under low-visibility merge conditions and recommended delaying release until the simulator and road tests matched. Leadership approved deployment before that validation was complete.
witnesses.reliabilityNotes: Directly involved in safety review, though frustrated by internal escalation failures.

witnesses.name: Leon Kwan
witnesses.statement: Engineering told us the latest patch reduced the braking issue to a rare edge case. The launch followed the roadmap we believed met the required internal thresholds.
witnesses.reliabilityNotes: Central to the approval decision, but motivated to frame the launch as reasonable.

witnesses.name: Dr. Sofia Mendez
witnesses.statement: The risk memo described the same type of merge conflict that later occurred in the crash. The probability estimate was uncertain, but the hazard itself was not speculative.
witnesses.reliabilityNotes: Technically strong and independent, though she reviewed only sampled internal materials.

evidence.description: A pre-launch memo warning that the braking stack could underperform when sensor confidence fell during merge maneuvers.
evidence.relevance: Shows advance notice of the same failure mode implicated in the crash.

evidence.description: Messages documenting executive approval of deployment after discussion of timeline pressure and patch confidence.
evidence.relevance: Links decision-makers to the final release despite unresolved concerns.

evidence.description: Recorded braking hesitation and confidence oscillation in the seconds before impact.
evidence.relevance: Provides technical support that the collision followed the warned-about braking pattern.

priorRulings.title: State v. Carris Systems
priorRulings.relevance: A negligence case can proceed when leadership had notice of a concrete safety hazard and still authorized deployment into public use.

prosecutionObjective: Show executives knew of a specific braking hazard, accepted the public safety risk, and launched anyway under commercial pressure.
defenseObjective: Show the technical risk was not fully understood, mitigation efforts were underway, and the prosecution cannot fairly assign criminal blame for a complex systems failure.
